data.title: Zepp Health Acquires Core Assets of Wild.AI to Expand Support for Female Athletes Across Life Stages
data.description:
(22:42 GMT) *Zepp Health Acquires Core Assets of Wild.AI
(22:45 GMT) Press Release: Zepp Health Acquires Core Assets of Wild.AI to Expand Support for Female Athletes Across Life Stages

MILPITAS, Calif.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--September 04, 2025--

Zepp Health , the parent company of Amazfit, today announced it has acquired the core assets and intellectual property of Wild.AI, a pioneering women's wellness platform focused on optimizing female performance through hormone-informed coaching. The acquisition was completed at the end of August and includes Wild.AI's proprietary technology and select members of its leadership team.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250904422306/en/

Zepp Health , the parent company of Amazfit, today announced it has acquired the core assets and intellectual property of Wild.AI, a pioneering women's wellness platform focused on optimizing female performance through hormone-informed coaching.

Wild.AI is renowned for using physiological and hormonal data to help women Track, Train, Fuel, and Recover based on their cycle and life stage. From menstruation to menopause, the platform delivers personalized insights that help improve performance, reduce injury risk, and deepen body awareness--serving both elite athletes and everyday fitness enthusiasts.

This acquisition builds on a successful partnership between Zepp Health and Wild.AI, including the recent launch of a Wild.AI mini-app on Amazfit's Active 2 smartwatch. That collaboration underscored a shared commitment to science-backed, user-centric performance tools.

"Our mantra of Train, Recover, Repeat found natural alignment with Wild.AI's mission," said Scott Shepley, Head of Global Marketing, Amazfit. "Rather than reinventing the wheel, we saw an opportunity to make it run smoother and faster--by integrating Wild.AI's cutting-edge capabilities into our performance ecosystem. This is a major step forward in delivering meaningful value to female athletes across the world."

"Wild.AI was born from a desire to build for women first--not as an afterthought," said Hélène Guillaume, Founder of Wild.AI. "Zepp Health shares our belief that physiology-informed tools are critical for women to thrive in sport. Together, we'll continue to support our community and bring Wild.AI's science-driven approach to millions more."

Wild.AI App Key Capabilities:

  • Daily hormone-aware symptom tracking to power individualized insights
  • Integration with wearables (including Amazfit, Garmin, Apple Watch) for enhanced data
  • Adaptive training and nutrition guidance based on hormonal shifts, symptoms, and recovery status
  • Educational content that helps users understand hormonal impacts on performance, recovery, and injury risk
  • Trend analysis and monthly reports that surface correlations between training load, symptoms, and hormonal phases

As Zepp Health expands its offering to better serve the female athlete community, the company will:

  • Continue supporting the existing Wild.AI app for current users across iOS and Android
  • Maintain compatibility with third-party wearables such as Apple Watch and Garmin
  • Begin global rollout of Wild.AI features across Amazfit devices, starting in the United States
  • Deepen its focus on hormone-informed coaching and wellness across all stages of a woman's life

The Wild.AI app will remain available for non-Amazfit users, and all existing services will continue uninterrupted.

About Amazfit

Amazfit, a leading global smart wearable brand focused on health and fitness, is part of Zepp Health , a health technology company with its principal office based in Gorinchem, the Netherlands. Zepp Health operates as a distributed organization, with team members and offices across the Americas, Europe, Asia, and other global markets.

Offering a wide selection of smartwatches and bands, Amazfit's brand tagline, "Discover Amazing," encourages individuals to break barriers, exceed expectations, and find joy in every moment. Amazfit is powered by Zepp Health's proprietary health management platform, which delivers cloud-based, 24/7 actionable insights and guidance to help users achieve their wellness goals.

Known for outstanding craftsmanship, Amazfit smartwatches have won numerous design awards, including the iF Design Award and the Red Dot Design Award. Launched in 2015, Amazfit is embraced by millions of users, with products available in over 90 countries across the Americas, EMEA, and APAC regions. For more information, visit www.amazfit.com.

About Wild.AI

Wild.AI helps women turn their hormones into their superpower through an ecosystem of groundbreaking products. Their flagship product, the Wild.AI app, is dedicated to helping women enhance their fitness and sports performance through insights tailored to the main milestones in a woman's life - menstrual cycle (including 148 birth controls), perimenopause, and menopause. The app, backed by 450 whitepapers, offers practical training, nutrition, and recovery advice to optimize health and fitness outcomes. Wild.AI also offers a comprehensive suite of additional tools, including a coaching dashboard, a coaching academy, and a supplement store designed to enhance women's health and performance.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250904422306/en/

CONTACT: Media Contact

Mary Woodbury

Head of Public Relations, Amazfit North America

mary.woodbury@zepp.com

data.title: Medibank Says Hey, McIntyre to Stand for Election as Nonexecutive Directors at AGM
Corn, soybean basis bids mostly steady
data.description:
Spot basis bids for corn and soybeans were mostly steady at river terminals and grain elevators in the U.S. Midwest on Thursday, as according to a spot check of locations.

  • Grain merchants at several Midwest elevators and processors said that farmer selling had slowed as prices slumped, particularly among wheat farmers.

  • An Illinois flour mill lowered its wheat basis by more than the 17-cent spread between the September (WU25) and December (WZ25) CBOT wheat futures contracts, in effect lowering their cash wheat bid.

  • Some grain facilities continued to roll their corn and wheat basis bids to the December futures contract (CZ25), (WZ25) from the September (CU25), (WU25) contract.

  • U.S. soybean futures rebounded on late-session technical buying to end firm on Thursday after concerns about a lack of sales to top importer China had dragged the market to 3-1/2 week lows.

  • Meanwhile, Chicago Board of Trade corn futures saw technical support, while wheat fell to fresh contract lows on ample global supplies.

data.title: PRES TRUMP TO SIGN EXECUTIVE ORDER TOMORROW CHANGING THE NAME OF THE DEFENSE DEPARTMENT TO THE DEPARTMENT OF WAR - CNN
IAG's RAC Insurance Deal Unlikely to Proceed — Market Talk
data.description: IAG's proposed acquisition of RAC Insurance is unlikely to proceed after the Australian competition regulator raised concerns about the deal, Morgan Stanley says. It estimates the combined share of IAG and RAC in Western Australia's motor and home insurance market could approach 50%. That is likely too high. "We think the debate starts to shift back to organic growth," says analyst Andrei Stadnik. IAG's top-line growth guidance for FY 2026 — when excluding benefits from acquisitions — is slightly softer than peers. "With less support from inorganic opportunities and fewer reinsurance levers to pull, we see IAG's earnings growth moderating alongside a slower pricing cycle," MS says. It has an equal-weight call on IAG. (david.winning@wsj.com)

Sine, Chief Strategy & Legal Officer at GoDaddy, sold 836 shares of Class A Common Stock on September 2 and 3, 2025, at prices of $146.26 and $142.21 per share, respectively, totaling $120,811. Following these transactions, Sine directly owns 65,577 shares of the company's Class A Common Stock.

Brian Sharples, a Director at GoDaddy, sold 500 shares of Class A Common Stock on September 2, 2025, at a price of $146.26 per share, totaling $73,130. After this sale, Sharples directly owns 20,957 shares of GoDaddy's Class A Common Stock.

data.title: Lotus Resources's Capital Raise Appears Prudent — Market Talk
data.description: Lotus Resources's A$65 million capital raise looks a sensible move to Ord Minnett. Lotus wants to fortify its balance sheet as it ramps up output from the Kayelekera uranium mine in Malawi. Analyst Matthew Hope had felt Lotus's finances were strong enough to manage the restart of Kayelekera. But he says Lotus is raising from a position of strength because its share price is up some 43% over the prior month. "We consider Lotus's approach prudent, as it will consume working capital in December half of 2025 with the commencement of mining and growth of product and run-of-mine inventories," Ord Minnett says. "Cash receipts are most likely due in June half of 2026." (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Mexico's Banorte to sell digital bank Bineo to fintech Klar
data.description:
Mexican lender Banorte will sell its digital bank unit Bineo to financial technology firm Klar, it said in a filing on Thursday, giving the young startup access to its much-coveted banking license.

Klar had applied for a license of its own late last year, though obtaining one via company acquisition has become a common way for would-be banks in Mexico to avoid years of red tape.

Banorte executives had said in April they would scrap Bineo as part of a larger overhaul of the lender's digital strategy. The unit failed to turn a profit, and CEO Marcos Ramirez told journalists that Banorte could sell the banking license for some $100 million.

The lender on Thursday did not give financial details for the transaction, which is dependent on approval from regulators.

Klar did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Hong Kong's Fosun to divest stake in Luz Saúde to Macquarie-linked firm
data.description:
Hong Kong's Fosun International said on Friday its unit had agreed to sell a 40% stake in its Portuguese-based hospital operator Luz Saúde, S.A for 310 million euros ($362.92 million) to an entity linked to Australian conglomerate Macquarie Group .

($1 = 0.8542 euros)

Trump administration considering banning trans people from buying guns, US media reports
data.description:
The U.S. Justice Department is considering proposals to prevent transgender people from buying guns, U.S. media reported on Thursday.

CNN, citing unnamed sources, described talks on the matter as "preliminary in nature" and said they stemmed from last week's shooting at a Catholic church in Minnesota, which left two children dead and 17 others injured. The suspect in the shooting was transgender, according to Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem.

The New York Post, also citing unnamed sources, also reported that talks were under way.

"The DOJ is actively evaluating options to prevent the pattern of violence we have seen from individuals with specific mental health challenges and substance abuse disorders. No specific criminal justice proposals have been advanced at this time," a Justice Department spokesperson said when asked about the media reports.

The talks within President Donald Trump's administration would pit the Republican Party's long-held support of gun rights against the administration's moves to limit the rights of transgender Americans.

Trump previously signed executive orders banning transgender people from the military and mandating that identification documents issued by the government be based on "an individual’s immutable biological classification as either male or female."

The Gun Owners of America rights group in a social media post responding to CNN's report said: "GOA opposes any & all gun bans. Full stop."

Although the Second Amendment of the U.S. Constitution protects the right for U.S. citizens to bear arms, there are several exceptions, including convicts and anyone declared as mentally "defective," according to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives.

data.title: 'Alligator Alcatraz' operations may continue, divided appeals court rules
data.description:
Key points:
  • Court rules Trump administration likely to win against environmental groups
  • Facility construction did not require federal environmental review, court says
  • DHS says lawsuit aims to hinder law enforcement, not protect the environment

A federal appeals court on Thursday allowed the federal migrant detention center known as "Alligator Alcatraz" to resume operations, blocking a lower court ruling that ordered the facility to stop taking new detainees and halt construction work.

The 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in a split opinion that President Donald Trump's administration is likely to prevail in a legal battle with environmental groups who say the facility is endangering the Everglades and its wildlife. Two judges sided with the Trump administration, and one judge dissented.

The majority ruled that the project - which has been funded by the state of Florida - did not trigger the kind of environmental review needed for federally funded construction projects.

Although both Florida Governor Ron DeSantis and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem have said the federal government will pay for expanding the detention facility, there is no evidence that federal funds have been used for construction, the court ruled.

DeSantis said in a video posted to social media on Thursday that the detention facility was "open for business" and ready to support Trump's immigration enforcement efforts.

The U.S. Department of Homeland Security called the ruling a "huge victory" in a social media post.

"This lawsuit was never about the environmental impacts of turning a developed airport into a detention facility," DHS said. "It has and will always be about open-borders activists and judges trying to keep law enforcement from removing dangerous criminal aliens from our communities, full stop."

One of the environmental groups behind the lawsuit, Friends of the Everglades, said on its website that the detention center was hastily constructed and threatened environmental harm to a vital stretch of protected American wilderness.

The facility is located 37 miles (60 km) west of Miami in a vast subtropical wetland that is home to alligators, crocodiles and pythons - imagery that the White House leveraged to show its determination to remove migrants it says were wrongly allowed to stay in the U.S. under former President Joe Biden.

The detention center cost about $250 million to build, and covers over 18 acres (seven hectares) at a site that was formerly used as a "small but bustling working airport" in Miami-Dade County and Collier County, according to court documents. The reconstructed site could house thousands of detainees and has been used to detain 900 migrants so far, according to the environmental groups' lawsuit.

Two environmental groups filed a legal motion in June seeking to block further construction at the detention site, saying it violated federal, state, and local environmental laws.

Trump, who has toured the site, has dismissed the environmental concerns, saying the detention facility was a template for what he would like to do nationwide.

The Republican president, who has a home in Florida, has for a decade made aggressive immigration and border policies central to his political agenda.

data.title: Vault Minerals Could Spend A$180 Million on Buyback — Market Talk
data.description: Vault Minerals's surprise decision to buy back stock is a good "back-stop" mechanism for its share price, Euroz Hartleys says. Vault didn't put a dollar value on the buyback. It said the intention is to repurchase up to 10% of its share capital over 12 months. Analyst Mike Millikan notes that Vault's share price has risen some 36% to A$0.57 since the buyback was announced. He thinks Vault could spend A$180 million on repurchasing stock. Euroz Hartleys retains a buy call on Vault, and lifts its price target by 12% to A$0.75/share. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1829 ET [Dow Jones]--Vault Minerals's surprise decision to buy back stock is a good "back-stop" mechanism for its share price, Euroz Hartleys says. Vault didn't put a dollar value on the buyback. It said the intention is to repurchase up to 10% of its share capital over 12 months. Analyst Mike Millikan notes that Vault's share price has risen some 36% to A$0.57 since the buyback was announced. He thinks Vault could spend A$180 million on repurchasing stock. Euroz Hartleys retains a buy call on Vault, and lifts its price target by 12% to A$0.75/share. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1752 ET - Australia's recent company reporting season hinted at improving consumer demand in Australia and New Zealand, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Parsing data from the past five weeks, Michael Simotas and Naveed Fazal Bawa observe that the period was volatile for stocks, but largely positive for those in consumer sectors. They say that 10 out of 13 companies they cover saw their shares outperform the broader market across the reporting season, with furniture retailers Harvey Norman and Nick Scali the best performers. They see further upside potential for Harvey Norman, Nick Scali and supermarket supplier Metcash, on the expectation that current consumer-demand trends will continue. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1744 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set to extend its late-week rally after hopes of a Fed rate cut helped lift U.S. equities. ASX futures are up by 0.4% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the benchmark index will add to the 1.0% rise that trimmed its deficit so far this week to 1.6%. The ASX still looks almost certain to snap a run of four consecutive weekly gains. In the U.S., the S&P 500 gained 0.8% and closed at a record as the release of weaker employment data was seen as supporting the case for the Fed to cut later this month. The DJIA added 0.8% and the Nasdaq Composite put on 1.0%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1742 ET - Consumers are spending less on performance activewear, and apparel overall, as they grow more selective in their purchases, seeking out truly new styles, CEO Calvin McDonald says during a call with analysts. As a result, the company's aims to reinvigorate demand with new seasonal colors in its core product lineup hasn't played out as expected, he says, though customers have responded well to its entirely new styles. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1734 ET - Lululemon has relied too heavily on its core franchises, like its scuba sweatshirts, a move that has caused sales to slow as consumers seek new products, CEO Calvin McDonald says during a call with analysts. "We have let our product life cycles run too long within many of our core categories," he says, noting the company has become too predictable within its casual offerings, missing opportunities to create new trends. The company now plans to add more new items at a faster pace, with plans for new styles to make up 35% of its total assortment in the spring, up from 23% currently, he says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1604 ET - U.S. stocks rise ahead of tomorrow's monthly payroll update. ADP's private-employment report shows fewer new jobs than expected in August, while weekly jobless claims are slightly higher than forecast. Markets are pricing in a rate cut later this month, largely tied to a weakening labor market. Consumer discretionary stocks climb the most, helped by some strong retailer earnings. All members of the Magnificent 7 stocks rise. DJIA gains 350 points, or 0.8%, to 45621, the S&P 500 rises 0.8% to 6502 and the Nasdaq climbs 1% to 21707. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1543 ET - Boeing says it could replace striking St. Louis-area machinists as the deadlock between the two sides moves into its second month. The aerospace company says contingency plans for its fighter-jet and munitions production lines are working, though "requisitions for permanent replacement workers" are being posted ahead of a virtual job fair Sept. 16. Roughly 3,200 IAM members walked off in early August over dissatisfaction with wage and benefits offers. The union on Thursday said "the ball is in Boeing's court" to restart contract talks. (andrew.fitzgerald@wsj.com; @drewfitzgerald)

1355 ET - Figma has several growth drivers that have yet to play out, including the monetization of its prompt-based AI design offering, Figma Make, William Blair analysts say in a note. The feature has garnered promising early feedback after its recent launch, but the company has yet to monetize it and seems to be taking a conservative approach to guidance, the analysts say. Figma will likely be able to monetize the offering heading into 2026, driving upside to current estimates, the analysts say. "We believe this offering is critical to the future of the company and are encouraged by the good early uptake," they say, reiterating their outperform rating on the stock. Figma sinks 20% to $54.74 after sales guidance missed some analyst views. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1344 ET - Advanced Micro Devices is struggling to grow orders from its customers, says Seaport analyst Jay Goldberg, who downgrades the stock to neutral from buy. The chipmaker is improving its MI series accelerators, but "many of their headline customers have only purchased evaluation systems that are unlikely to convert into volume orders for at least one generation of the MI systems," Goldberg says. Customers like Microsoft and Meta may also reevaluate their AI spending plans, leading to reduced orders. The company's use of discounts is also becoming more widespread and it may lose negotiating leverage with suppliers, hurting margins, Goldberg says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1330 ET - Figma's 2Q results show the company is a leader in the growing user interface and user experience design industry, BofA Securities says in a note. The company looks well positioned to continue gaining share in the $36 billion digital design marketplace given its competitive advantages like a large base of monthly active users and paid customers, as well as its comprehensive offerings spanning whiteboarding, development, websites and social media, the analysts say. It also has a powerful set of generative AI features, which could drive further growth next year with monetization, they say. Still, the analysts lower their price target on the stock to $69 from $85 given revenue growth is decelerating, though it remains at a double-digit rate. Figma sinks 18% to $55.58. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1315 ET - Salesforce's 2Q results are unlikely to change any views about the company, including concerns its AI investments may not be paying off enough, according to Oppenheimer. Third-quarter guidance "reveals a continuing tough macro environment," the analysts say, and "is unlikely to catalyze new opinions." The underwhelming sales outlook on the lighter end of expectations, is creating some restlessness among investors, Truist analyst Terry Tillman says. The 2Q report indicates that front-office AI is still in its very early stages, UBS analysts Karl Keirstead and Dean Marriott say, maintaining a neutral rating until there's a clearer path to more growth. Salesforce slides 5%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1240 ET - Homebuilder D.R. Horton doesn't expect its margins to improve without a change in the housing environment, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless, citing a conversation with the company's management. The company sees opportunities to reduce labor costs while appreciation on land and development costs have slowed. But the incentives the company is offering to sell homes are still higher than historical levels, with affordability concerns from buyers and excess home inventory in some markets forcing builders to offer mortgage buydowns. "Netting it together, management does not anticipate gross margins can grow in the current environment without some help on the incentive front," McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Meta Platforms Executives Sell Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Olivan JavierChief Operating OfficerSell$375,471Form 4
Newstead JenniferChief Legal OfficerSell$376,923Form 4

Meta Platforms' Chief Operating Officer, Olivan Javier, and Chief Legal Officer, Jennifer Newstead, have both sold shares of the company's Class A Common Stock on September 2, 2025.

Olivan Javier sold 517 shares at a price of $726.25 per share, totaling $375,471. Following this transaction, Javier directly owns 12,094 shares and indirectly owns 110,736 shares through various entities and trusts.

Jennifer Newstead sold 519 shares at the same price of $726.25 per share, totaling $376,923. After the sale, Newstead directly owns 31,908 shares of Meta Platforms. Her sale was conducted under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on February 11, 2025.

data.title: Cerence AI Files Lawsuit Against Apple, Alleging Patent Infringement
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Cerence AI filed a lawsuit against Apple, alleging the technology giant was using its technology in certain iPhones and iPads without seeking a license.

In the lawsuit filed Thursday in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Texas, Cerence alleged Apple was still using its technology--including text input and recognition and voice-command monitoring--in products with iOS13+.

Cerence said it contacted Apple in May 2021 about a potential acquisition, but nothing came of the discussions. Cerence provided Apple with mobile text entry patents and claim charts showing how Apple allegedly infringed on several patents.

Cerence is seeking damages and licensing fees from Apple.

Apple didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com
